Okay, in this session, our final session, Dan is going to reveal what should go into your referral toolkit and how to do wildly effective client referral events and how to get referrals from unlikely sources you probably never thought about before. So Dan, let's start with talking about something that people don't usually do. Actually, let's start with what should be in the referral toolbox. Let's start with there, then we're going to get to some more sophisticated strategies, which is the events. Well, you want to give clients a variety of tools that they can use to bring people to you, refer people to you, refer people to your information. And to a great extent, the more tools, the better. If you have a book that is informational and leads to you, you definitely want to give them two or three or four or six copies. Just like Dr. Brilson did we show it earlier. Exactly. If you have a DVD, a couple of DVDs. If you have a CD, a couple of CDs. If you are driving to a static standardized online presentation, then you certainly give them some cards, something that is pocket sized, glove compartment sized, etc. that drives to that presentation. Seven things every parent needs to know about. Every month you sort of restock them with those lead generation cards, hand out pieces, whatever that drive to the report of the month, the online video of the month, and so forth. So a lot of it is what we went in. I mean, if you just pick the pieces that we put in there, you want some entertainment in there, you talk about things like that, things that are fun. So how do you train the clients though on how to use these? So the first training is the overriding understanding that if you are a customer, a client, or a patient here, one of the things you do is refer. And we have, we are now giving them tools to do that with. It is helpful if you have a scripted, here's what to do conversation that occurs between you and customer client patient, or between your staff and customer client patient, about a primary means of referring. So we often have in healthcare, in financial services, there will be the book and the shock and awe package, the literature package put together into some sort of convenient, sort of doesn't fall all over the place. I like clear plastic bags myself. And you might get three of them. Usually like after your welcome kit, say by the way, clients around here refer and often ask us, so here's all you have to do, here's three kits. You can keep them in a trunk of your car, you can keep them at home, you can keep it in the office, whatever. And anytime you have a conversation with somebody who has chronic headaches, has a crap load of money, doesn't know what to do with it, whatever. All you have to do is give them one of these and say here, this is a really interesting book and some information from Billy Bob who manages our money. He's the guy who manages our money. I think you'll really find this book interesting. That's all you have to do. And if you say it like three times, they memorize it, they get it. Some people will give them a manual that literally is here's how to refer people to us. And here's, so if you look at his map of the box, think of a map, here's the five tools we gave you. Here's how to use them. Here's how to use them. Some will give them here's who we want and here's who we don't want. So here's who we want, here's who we don't want. So the important premise is that this is the opposite of entitlement expectation. This is nothing good is going to happen automatically. We've got to make it happen. And it is the fact that customers, clients and patients can be trained. They can be trained about how they're supposed to behave in every respect, whether you're supposed to be here on time or not, whether you're supposed to do your stuff. And that behavior includes, you're supposed to refer and here's how we do this around here. One of the key things that you didn't say though, which I know is a staff though. Your staff needs to be trained.
